title: Polynomials & Factoring
blurb: Terms, degree, and standard form; adding, subtracting, and multiplying polynomials; factoring from GCF to trinomials — and solving by the zero product.

lessons.title: Meet the Polynomials
lessons.concreteComparison: A box's volume might be $x^3$, its surface might involve $x^2$, its edges $x$ — and a flat fee tacks on a constant. Add such pieces and you get expressions like $x^3 + 6x^2 - 2x + 7$: a **polynomial**. They're the workhorses of algebra — every quadratic you'll solve this year, every parabola you'll graph, is one of these.
lessons.objective: Name a polynomial's terms, coefficients, and degree; write polynomials in standard form; and classify by degree and term count.
concept: A **polynomial** is a sum of **terms**, each a number times a whole-number power of $x$: $3x^2 - 5x + 7$ has terms $3x^2$, $-5x$, $7$. The numbers $3, -5, 7$ are **coefficients**.
The **degree** is the highest power: $3x^2-5x+7$ has degree $2$ (a **quadratic**). Degree 1 is **linear**, degree 3 **cubic**; a lone constant has degree 0.
**Standard form** orders terms by falling degree: $7 - 5x + 3x^2$ becomes $3x^2 - 5x + 7$. The first coefficient ($3$) is the **leading coefficient**.
Counting terms: 1 term = monomial, 2 = binomial, 3 = trinomial. And evaluation is just substitution: $3x^2-5x+7$ at $x=2$ is $3(4)-10+7=9$.
lessons.keyIdea: Polynomial = sum of coefficient·xⁿ terms; degree = highest power; standard form = falling powers.

workedExamples.statement: Write $7 - 5x + 3x^2$ in standard form and give its degree and leading coefficient.
workedExamples.solution: $3x^2 - 5x + 7$; degree $2$, leading coefficient $3$. (Same values at every $x$: e.g. $x=1$ gives $5$ both ways.)

workedExamples.statement: Classify $4x^3 - x$ by degree and by number of terms.

commonMistakes.text: Taking the degree from the FIRST term as written.
commonMistakes.correction: $7 + x^5$ has degree 5, not 0 — the degree is the HIGHEST power anywhere, so put it in standard form first.

commonMistakes.text: Dropping a term's sign when reordering.
commonMistakes.correction: $7 - 5x + 3x^2 \to 3x^2 - 5x + 7$: the minus stays glued to the $5x$.

steps.title: Terms, coefficients, degree
beats: Whole-number powers only — that's the club rule.

steps.title: Standard form
beats: Now the degree ($2$) leads the parade,

steps.title: Evaluating
beats: A polynomial is a machine — feed it $x$, read $y$.

examples.prompt: Standard form, degree, and value at $x=1$ of $2x - x^3 + 5$?

problems.prompt: The leading coefficient of $4 - x^2 + 3x$ (in standard form) is…

steps.title: Why “polynomial”?
steps.body: Greek **poly** (many) + Latin **nomen** (name/term): “many terms.” The family is ancient — Babylonian scribes solved quadratics on clay 4,000 years ago — but the tidy notation you're using is barely 400 years old. You write algebra more clearly than Newton did as a student.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Terms, coefficients, and degree name the polynomial.
Standard form: powers falling left to right.
Evaluate by substituting — a machine like any function.

lessons.title: Adding & Subtracting Polynomials
lessons.concreteComparison: Two food stalls tally their day: stall one made $2x^2+3x-5$ tugriks (where $x$ is the hour count), stall two made $x^2-x+2$. The combined haul? Just merge the like pieces: the $x^2$s together, the $x$s together, the constants together — $3x^2+2x-3$. Polynomials add the way coins do: sort by denomination first.
lessons.objective: Add and subtract polynomials by combining like terms — and distribute the minus through every term when subtracting.
concept: **Like terms** share the exact same power of $x$: $2x^2$ and $x^2$ combine; $2x^2$ and $3x$ never do.
**Adding**: group like powers and add coefficients. $(2x^2+3x-5) + (x^2-x+2) = 3x^2+2x-3$.
**Subtracting**: the minus hits EVERY term of the second polynomial. $(2x^2+3x-5) - (x^2-x+2) = 2x^2+3x-5-x^2+x-2 = x^2+4x-7$.
Sanity-check with a number: at $x=4$, the originals give $39$ and $14$; the sum $3x^2+2x-3$ gives $53 = 39+14$ ✓.
lessons.keyIdea: Sort by power, combine coefficients; subtraction distributes the minus to every term.

commonMistakes.correction: $x^2$ and $x$ are different denominations — they sit side by side, uncombined.

commonMistakes.text: Flipping only the first sign when subtracting.
commonMistakes.correction: $-(x^2 - x + 2)$ becomes $-x^2 + x - 2$ — all three signs flip, not just the first.

steps.title: Subtracting
beats: the minus hits ALL of $x^2$, $-x$, and $+2$.

steps.title: Merge the polynomials
steps.intro: Group by power; watch subtraction signs.

steps.title: The substitution lie detector
steps.body: Not sure your algebra is right? Feed both sides a number. If $(2x^2+3x-5)+(x^2-x+2)$ really equals $3x^2+2x-3$, they must agree at EVERY $x$ — so try $x=4$: $39+14=53$ ✓. One mismatch and you've caught a mistake. This platform machine-checks every answer exactly this way.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Subtraction flips EVERY sign in the second polynomial.
Spot-check with a number — algebra can't hide.

lessons.title: Multiplying Polynomials
lessons.concreteComparison: A garden is $x+2$ meters by $x+3$ meters. Its area isn't $x^2+6$ — picture the plot cut into four patches: $x\cdot x$, $x\cdot 3$, $2\cdot x$, and $2\cdot 3$. Add them: $x^2+5x+6$. Multiplying binomials is just the area of a rectangle, patch by patch — every term shakes hands with every term.
lessons.objective: Multiply a monomial into a polynomial and multiply binomials (every-term-by-every-term), collecting like terms.
concept: **Monomial × polynomial**: distribute. $3x(x+2) = 3x^2 + 6x$.
**Binomial × binomial**: EVERY term of the first multiplies EVERY term of the second — four products. $(x+2)(x+3) = x^2 + 3x + 2x + 6 = x^2+5x+6$.
The middle terms usually merge: $3x+2x=5x$. Notice $5 = 2+3$ and $6 = 2\cdot3$ — the shortcut behind factoring, coming two lessons from now.
Signs ride along: $(x+2)(x-5) = x^2 -5x +2x -10 = x^2 -3x -10$.
lessons.keyIdea: Every term multiplies every term (four products for binomials); then combine the middles.

facts.title: The pattern
facts.explanation: Sum in the middle, product at the end.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying only the firsts and lasts: $(x+2)(x+3)=x^2+6$.
commonMistakes.correction: That skips the two middle handshakes ($3x$ and $2x$). All FOUR products, always.

commonMistakes.text: Sign slips in the middle terms.
commonMistakes.correction: $(x+2)(x-5)$: the middles are $-5x$ and $+2x$, giving $-3x$ — track each product's sign separately.

steps.title: Multiplication is area
beats: Slice it: an $x\times x$ patch, an $x\times3$ strip,
a $2\times x$ strip, and a $2\times3$ corner.

steps.title: Four handshakes
steps.explanation: $x\cdot x + x\cdot4 + 1\cdot x + 1\cdot4$: the middles merge to $5x$.

steps.title: Sum and product
beats: factoring will run it in REVERSE.

steps.title: FOIL is just a mnemonic
steps.body: Firsts, Outers, Inners, Lasts — FOIL — names the four binomial handshakes. But the AREA picture is mightier: it survives trinomials, where FOIL breaks down and the garden simply grows more patches. Understand the plot, not the acronym.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Signs travel with their terms through every product.

lessons.title: Special Products
lessons.concreteComparison: Two multiplications appear so often they deserve reflexes. Square a binomial — $(x+3)^2$ — and a middle term ALWAYS shows up: $x^2+6x+9$, never $x^2+9$. Multiply conjugates — $(x+3)(x-3)$ — and the middles annihilate: $x^2-9$, clean. These two patterns power mental math, factoring, and half of the algebra ahead.
lessons.objective: Expand squares of binomials $(a\pm b)^2$ and conjugate products $(a+b)(a-b)$ instantly — and never drop the middle term.
concept: **Square of a sum**: $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$. The middle term $2ab$ is the two cross-handshakes: $(x+3)^2 = x^2+6x+9$.
**Square of a difference**: $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$. Only the middle goes negative: $(x-4)^2 = x^2-8x+16$.
**Conjugates** (same terms, opposite sign): $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$. The middles cancel: $(x+3)(x-3) = x^2-9$ — the **difference of squares**.
lessons.keyIdea: $(a\pm b)^2 = a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2$ (middle never vanishes); $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2-b^2$ (middle always vanishes).

workedExamples.statement: Expand $(x+3)(x-3)$ — and compute $21\cdot19$ the clever way.

commonMistakes.correction: Squaring is NOT distributable over +. The middle $2\cdot3\cdot x = 6x$ always appears: $x^2+6x+9$. (Test $x=1$: $16 \neq 10$.)
$(x+3)(x-3)$ has middles $-3x+3x=0$ — the answer is exactly $x^2-9$, nothing between.

steps.title: No shortcuts
steps.explanation: The middle is $2\cdot4\cdot x = 8x$ — squaring never skips it.

steps.title: Conjugates
beats: The **difference of squares**: two terms only.

steps.title: Specials on sight
steps.intro: Name the pattern before you expand.

steps.title: The most common mistake in algebra
steps.body: Math educators have actually studied it: $(a+b)^2 = a^2+b^2$ is among the most-committed algebra errors on Earth — so common it has a nickname, “the freshman's dream.” You now hold the antidote: the middle term $2ab$ comes from two real handshakes, and it never skips the party.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $(a\pm b)^2 = a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2$ — three terms, always.
Conjugates turn hard arithmetic into instant math.

lessons.title: Factoring
lessons.concreteComparison: Expanding is grinding coffee — easy, one direction. **Factoring** is un-grinding: given $x^2+5x+6$, recover the $(x+2)(x+3)$ it came from. Why bother? Because a factored polynomial confesses its secrets — where it's zero, how it behaves — and next lesson those confessions solve equations.
lessons.objective: Factor out the GCF, factor trinomials $x^2+bx+c$ by sum-and-product, and factor differences of squares.
concept: **GCF first, always**: $3x^2+6x = 3x(x+2)$ — pull out the biggest shared factor before anything else.
**Trinomials $x^2+bx+c$**: hunt two numbers with sum $b$ and product $c$. For $x^2+5x+6$: sum 5, product 6 → $2$ and $3$ → $(x+2)(x+3)$.
**Signs tell you where to look**: $x^2-5x+6$ → both negative: $(x-2)(x-3)$. $x^2+x-6$ → opposite signs: $(x+3)(x-2)$.
**Difference of squares**: $x^2-9 = (x+3)(x-3)$ — the conjugate pattern in reverse. And always CHECK by expanding (or by substituting a number).
lessons.keyIdea: GCF first; then two numbers with sum b and product c; a²−b² splits into conjugates.

workedExamples.solution: Sum 5, product 6: the pair is 2 and 3 → $(x+2)(x+3)$. Check at $x=4$: $42=6\cdot7$ ✓.

commonMistakes.correction: $2x^2+10x+12$: pull the 2 FIRST → $2(x^2+5x+6) = 2(x+2)(x+3)$. Without it, the sum-product hunt fails.
$x^2-5x+6$ needs sum $-5$, product $+6$: BOTH negative, $(x-2)(x-3)$ — positive product means matching signs.

steps.title: What factoring is
beats: Factored form reveals where the polynomial is ZERO.

steps.title: Sum and product
steps.prompt: To factor $x^2+7x+12$, you need two numbers with…
steps.explanation: Sum = middle coefficient, product = constant: $3$ and $4$ → $(x+3)(x+4)$.

steps.title: Where to hunt
beats: The constant's sign decides matching vs opposite.

steps.title: Factoring guards your passwords
steps.body: Multiplying two enormous primes is instant; factoring the result back apart is so hard that internet encryption (RSA) rests on it. Your bank login is protected by exactly the asymmetry you just felt: expanding is easy, factoring is work.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $x^2+bx+c$: two numbers, sum $b$, product $c$; signs guide the hunt.

lessons.title: Solving by the Zero Product
lessons.concreteComparison: Multiply two numbers and get $0$ — what do you know? At least one of them WAS $0$. There's no other way. That innocent fact is a master key: to solve $x^2+x-6=0$, factor it into $(x+3)(x-2)=0$, and the key turns — $x+3=0$ or $x-2=0$, so $x=-3$ or $x=2$. Factoring just became an equation-solving machine.
lessons.objective: Solve quadratic equations by factoring and applying the zero-product property — after moving everything to one side.
concept: **Zero-product property**: if $A \cdot B = 0$ then $A=0$ or $B=0$. Nothing else multiplies to zero.
**The method**: get $=0$ on one side → factor → set each factor to zero → solve each. $x^2+x-6=0 \to (x+3)(x-2)=0 \to x=-3$ or $x=2$.
**Quadratics usually have TWO solutions** — report both, and check both: $(-3)^2+(-3)-6=0$ ✓ and $2^2+2-6=0$ ✓.
**The =0 is not optional**: for $x^2+2x=15$, first move: $x^2+2x-15=0 \to (x+5)(x-3)=0 \to x=-5$ or $3$. The property only speaks about products equal to ZERO.
lessons.keyIdea: Move to =0, factor, set each factor to zero — a product is zero only when a factor is.

facts.title: Zero product
facts.explanation: The only way to multiply to zero.

workedExamples.solution: $x=2$ or $x=-5$ — each root kills one factor. Checks: $(0)(7)=0$ ✓, $(-7)(0)=0$ ✓.

commonMistakes.text: Using the property when the product isn't zero.
commonMistakes.correction: $(x-2)(x+5)=8$ does NOT give $x-2=8$ — the shortcut works only for $=0$. Expand, move everything over, refactor.
$x^2+x-6=0$ has TWO roots ($-3$ and $2$). A quadratic usually does — check and keep both.

steps.title: Zero-product property
beats: no pair of nonzero numbers multiplies to zero.
This is why factored form solves equations.

steps.title: Turn the key
steps.explanation: Each factor gets its turn at zero: $x-4=0$ → 4; $x+1=0$ → $-1$.

steps.title: It must equal ZERO
beats: A product equal to 15 tells you nothing.

steps: $x=0$ or $x=6$ — don't divide away the $x=0$ root!

problems.prompt: A ball's height is $h=x(8-x)$. It's on the ground ($h=0$) when…

steps.title: You crack quadratics now
steps.body: Name, combine, expand, factor, and solve — the full polynomial toolkit for the equations ahead.

steps.title: You've finished “Polynomials & Factoring”
steps.body: Every skill here feeds the next topic: **Quadratic Equations**, where completing the square and the famous quadratic formula join your zero-product key — and no quadratic on Earth stays unsolved.

testYourself.statement: Standard form and degree of $3 - x + 2x^3$?
